title: Chelsea Twin Bed or Bottom Bunk
summary: A twin bed with a shaped jigsaw-cut 2x6 header, panel headboard and footboard, and turned feet. Works as a standalone bed or the bottom of a bunk stack.
description:
This is a twin bed with a bit of shape to it: a curved 2x6 header cut with a jigsaw crowns both the tall headboard and the shorter footboard, each built as a plywood panel framed by 2x2 legs and a 1x6 base trim, standing on turned hardwood feet. It reads as a proper piece of furniture, and it's dimensioned to double as the bottom bunk of a matching bunk stack.

The jigsaw header cut is the part to practice first. It's a 2x6 — thicker stock than a jigsaw loves — so test your cut on a scrap, go slow, and sand the finished curve smooth. A ragged header is the first thing anyone sees.

The structure is reassuringly forgiving. The 2x2 legs attach to the panels with pocket holes (set for 3/4" stock), but this isn't a high-stress joint — the load travels from the side rails into the legs and down, so glue and countersunk 3" screws work just as well if you skip the jig. The side rails carry the mattress load via inner cleats. A useful option: raise the cleats up to 1" from the top of the rail if you're using just a mattress (no box spring) so it sits at a comfortable height; drop them flush to the bottom if you have a box spring. The 1x3 slats screw to the cleats. Add the turned feet last, and finish or paint.

tools.note: Cuts the curved 2x6 header — practice on scrap first.
For the leg joints; countersunk 3" screws also work.

materials.name: Hardwood turned feet, 1-1/2 in dia, 6 in
1x3, 8 ft
materials.note: Optional slats (skip if using a box spring/bunkie board).

cutList.note: Cut the curved profile with a jigsaw.
Optional — skip with a box spring or bunkie board.

steps.title: Cut the shaped headers
steps.body: Cut the two 2x6 headers to the curved profile. This is harder than it looks with a jigsaw in thick 2x stock — test your cut on a scrap first, go slow when you're satisfied, and sand the finished edge smooth. A clean curve is what makes the bed.

steps.title: Attach the base trim to the panels
steps.body: On both the footboard and headboard panels, attach the 1x6 base trim to the bottom of the panel with 1-1/4" finish nails and glue.

steps.title: Attach the legs and header to the footboard
steps.body: Attach the 2x2 legs to the panel and header. Pocket holes set for 3/4" stock with 1-1/4" screws are recommended, but this isn't a high-stress joint — the load runs from the side rails to the legs and down — so glue and countersunk 3" screws work fine too.

steps.title: Build the headboard
steps.body: Build the headboard the same way as the footboard, with the taller legs and the larger panel, capped by its shaped header.

steps.title: Add the feet
steps.body: Add the turned decorative feet to the base of the headboard and footboard legs.
materials: Hardwood turned feet, 1-1/2 in dia, 6 in

steps.title: Add the cleats and side rails
steps.body: Attach the 2x2 cleats to the inside of the 1x6 side rails. If you're using just a mattress (no box spring), you can bring the cleat up — as much as 1" from the top — so the mattress sits higher; with a box spring, keep the cleats flush to the bottom of the rails. Join the rails to the headboard and footboard.

steps.title: Add the slats and finish
steps.body: Lay the 1x3 slats across the cleats and screw them down (skip this if you're using a box spring or bunkie board). Sand, fill holes, and finish or paint.

images.alt: Twin bed with a shaped header and panel headboard on turned feet
